elements.sent-2411-001: TrojanEdit can inject backdoors into image editing models using visual, textual, and multimodal triggers that combine both visual and textual inputs.
elements.sent-2411-002: TrojanEdit can achieve three different backdoor attack objectives: generating a preset image (Image-Attack), generating a preset style image (Style-Attack), and replacing a generated object with a preset object (Object-Attack).. \end{center ].}
elements.sent-2411-003: As diffusion models have achieved success in image generation tasks, many studies have extended them to other related fields like image editing.
elements.sent-2411-004: Unlike image generation, image editing aims to modify an image based on user requests while keeping other parts of the image unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-005: Among these, text-based image editing is the most representative task.
elements.sent-2411-006: Some studies have shown that diffusion models are vulnerable to backdoor attacks, where attackers may poison the training data to inject the backdoor into models.
elements.sent-2411-007: However, previous backdoor attacks on diffusion models primarily focus on image generation models without considering image editing models.
elements.sent-2411-008: Given that image editing models accept multimodal inputs, it raises a new question regarding the effectiveness of different modalities triggers in backdoor attacks on these models.
elements.sent-2411-009: To address this question, we propose a backdoor attack framework for image editing models, named TrojanEdit, which can handle different modalities triggers.
elements.sent-2411-010: We explore five types of visual triggers, three types of textual triggers, and combine them together as fifteen types of multimodal triggers, conducting extensive experiments for three types of backdoor attack goals.
elements.sent-2411-011: Our experimental results show that the image editing model has a backdoor bias for texture triggers.
elements.sent-2411-012: Compared to visual triggers, textual triggers have stronger attack effectiveness but also cause more damage to the model's normal functionality.
elements.sent-2411-013: Furthermore, we found that multimodal triggers can achieve a good balance between the attack effectiveness and model's normal functionality.
elements.sent-2411-014: Recently, diffusion models have achieved success in image generation~\cite{Dhariwal2021Diffusion,Nichol2022GLIDE:,Ramesh2022Hierarchical , and many studies have extended these models to other applications, such as image editing~\cite{Brooks2023InstructPix2Pix:,Kawar2022Imagic: , 3D generation~\cite{Zhou20213D,Luo2021Diffusion3D , and video generation~\cite{Ho2022Video,Ho2022ImagenVideoGeneration .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Unlike image generation, which takes a prompt as input and creates an image based on that prompt, image editing involves providing both an image and editing instructions.
elements.sent-2411-016: The output is a modified version of the original image, where the specified changes are applied while preserving all other unaltered elements.
elements.sent-2411-017: In image editing, text-based editing~\cite{Brooks2023InstructPix2Pix:,Kawar2022Imagic: is one of the most representative methods.}
elements.sent-2411-018: While diffusion models have demonstrated great capabilities, their vulnerabilities to backdoor attacks raise significant security concerns~\cite{Chou2023Howbackdoordm,Chou2023VillanDiffusionbackdoor,Vice2024BAGM:,Zhai2023Text-to-ImageEasilyBackdoored,Huang2024PersonalizationFew-ShotBackdoorDiffusion,Wang2024Thenbackdoordm .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Backdoor attacks typically involve poisoning the training data, causing the model to function normally with clean inputs but behave maliciously when exposed to triggered input.
elements.sent-2411-020: Chou~\etal~\cite{Chou2023Howbackdoordm proposed the first backdoor attack method for unconditional diffusion models~\cite{DDPM .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: By embedding a trigger in the input image and associating the triggered input images with target outputs during training, they inject a backdoor into the model.
elements.sent-2411-022: Later, Zhai~\etal~\cite{Zhai2023Text-to-ImageEasilyBackdoored extended this attack to text-to-image models by using a space in the prompt as the trigger.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Subsequent studies~\cite{Vice2024BAGM:,Ramesh2022Hierarchical have explored various methods of injecting backdoor into different components of text-to-image models.}
elements.sent-2411-024: However, prior studies~\cite{Chou2023Howbackdoordm,Chou2023VillanDiffusionbackdoor,Vice2024BAGM:,Zhai2023Text-to-ImageEasilyBackdoored,Huang2024PersonalizationFew-ShotBackdoorDiffusion,Wang2024Thenbackdoordm have primarily focused on image generation models, leaving image editing models unexplored.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Considering that image generation typically involves unimodal input, whereas image editing requires multimodal input, it is worthwhile to investigate the vulnerability of image editing models against backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-026: To the best of our knowledge, there has been no systematic study on backdoor attacks in image editing models.
elements.sent-2411-027: Therefore, we focus on backdoor attacks against text-based image editing models, one of the most representative models in image editing.
elements.sent-2411-028: The goal of backdoor attacks in image editing models is to make the model generate preset backdoor target images for triggered inputs while generating edited images normally for clean inputs.
elements.sent-2411-029: Unlike image generation models, backdoor attacks in image editing models accept multimodal inputs thereby raising a question: \begin{tcolorbox [colback=gray!10!white] How do different modalities of triggers perform within image editing models? \end{tcolorbox To address this problem, we propose a novel backdoor attack framework for image editing, named TrojanEdit.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Unlike previous backdoor attacks, TrojanEdit is specifically designed for multimodal inputs, introducing triggers through various combinations of text and image inputs.
elements.sent-2411-031: This allows TrojanEdit to flexibly combine existing triggers on both image and text inputs to achieve optimal backdoor attack effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-032: We considered 5 types of visual triggers~\cite{Gu2017BadNets:,blend,Nguyen2021WaNet,Jiang2023Colorbackdoor,redool and 3 types of textual triggers~\cite{Vice2024BAGM:,Zhai2023Text-to-ImageEasilyBackdoored , as well as and combine them together as fifteen types of multimodal triggers, to achieve 3 different attack goals in InstructPix2Pix~\cite{Brooks2023InstructPix2Pix: .}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Our experimental results show that using BadNets~\cite{Gu2017BadNets: can achieve the best performance in visual triggers, while the word trigger is most effective in textual.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The combination of BadNet and word triggers achieves the best results for multimodal triggers.
elements.sent-2411-035: The image editing model exhibits a backdoor bias for textual triggers.
elements.sent-2411-036: Compared to visual triggers, textual triggers are easier to use for attacking image editing models, but they also cause more damage to the model's normal functionality.
elements.sent-2411-037: Additionally, we found that multimodal triggers can balance attack effectiveness with the decline in the model's normal functionality.
elements.sent-2411-038: This is because, in image editing models, text directly guides image generation, while the original input image is primarily used to control the attention map to ensure that areas outside the edited region remain consistent with the original image.
elements.sent-2411-039: Adding a trigger to the text directly influences image generation, whereas a trigger on the image might be interpreted as a style or noise, potentially getting lost during the diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-040: Image editing aims to modify the given image to meet the specific requirements of users~\cite{Shuai2024SurveyofMultimodal-GuidedImageEditing .}
elements.sent-2411-041: This field initially relied on GANs to generate edited images~\cite{Xia2023GANSurvey , but as diffusion models~\cite{DDPM,Dhariwal2021DiffusionBeatgan have demonstrated significant advantages in image generation, many recent works have begun to be based on diffusion models~\cite{Brooks2023InstructPix2Pix:,Kawar2022Imagic: .}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Image editing techniques based on diffusion models can be multimodal-guided, including text~\cite{Hertz2022PrompttoPrompt,Tumanyan2023Plug-and-Play,Wang2024Thenbackdoordm,Nichol2022GLIDE:,Brooks2023InstructPix2Pix:,Kawar2022Imagic: , images~\cite{Avrahami2022Blended,lugmayr2022repaint , and user interfaces~\cite{shi2024dragdiffusion,epstein2023diffusion .}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: However, considering that it is more convenient and flexible for humans to describe specific purposes, text-based methods~\cite{Brooks2023InstructPix2Pix:,Kawar2022Imagic:,Hertz2022PrompttoPrompt are the most widely used in recent studies.}
elements.sent-2411-044: In this paper, we selected InstructPix2Pix~\cite{Brooks2023InstructPix2Pix: , a state-of-the-art text-based image editing model, as our target model.}
elements.sent-2411-045: InstructPix2Pix is capable of learning instructions and editing images based on those instructions.
elements.sent-2411-046: Backdoor attacks originally emerged in image classification tasks~\cite{Gu2017BadNets:,blend,Nguyen2021WaNet,Jiang2023Colorbackdoor,redool and were later extended to image generative tasks~\cite{Chou2023Howbackdoordm,Chou2023VillanDiffusionbackdoor,Vice2024BAGM:,Zhai2023Text-to-ImageEasilyBackdoored,Huang2024PersonalizationFew-ShotBackdoorDiffusion,Wang2024Thenbackdoordm .}}
elements.sent-2411-047: These attacks involve poisoning the training data, causing the model to perform normally on clean data but exhibit malicious behavior when specific triggers are present.
elements.sent-2411-048: The first backdoor attack on diffusion models was proposed by Chou et al.~\cite{Chou2023Howbackdoordm for image generation without conditional guidance.}
elements.sent-2411-049: In their approach, they embedded a trigger in the input image and trained the model on these triggered images, paired with target outputs.
elements.sent-2411-050: Later, Zhai et al.~\cite{Zhai2023Text-to-ImageEasilyBackdoored extended this attack to text-conditional guidance models by using a space in the text as the trigger.}
elements.sent-2411-051: In these backdoor-injected text-to-image models, a clean prompt generates a normal image, but if the prompt contains the trigger, the model generates a predetermined image.
elements.sent-2411-052: Subsequent studies~\cite{Vice2024BAGM:, Ramesh2022Hierarchical primarily focused on injecting backdoors into different parts of text-to-image models.}
elements.sent-2411-053: However, these backdoor attacks on diffusion models~\cite{Chou2023Howbackdoordm, Chou2023VillanDiffusionbackdoor, Vice2024BAGM:, Zhai2023Text-to-ImageEasilyBackdoored, Huang2024PersonalizationFew-ShotBackdoorDiffusion, Wang2024Thenbackdoordm have only considered injecting backdoors into image generation models, while image editing models have not been studied.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Given that image editing models differ from image generation models in that they accept multimodal inputs, thereby raising a question: How do different modalities of triggers perform within image editing models? Although there has been some research on multimodal backdoors, they have not focused on diffusion models~\cite{BackdooringMultimodalLearning, MultimodalBackdoorsQA .}
elements.sent-2411-055: Backdoor attacks on multimodal trigger diffusion models, such as image editing models, have yet to be explored.
elements.sent-2411-056: Attacker's capability Similar to backdoor attacks in previous classification models~\cite{Gu2017BadNets:,blend,Nguyen2021WaNet,Jiang2023Colorbackdoor,redool , we assume that the attacker can only poison the data and cannot manipulate the model's training process or access information such as the model's weights or gradients.}
elements.sent-2411-057: This is a classic scenario where the attacker conducts a backdoor attack primarily through data poisoning.
elements.sent-2411-058: Attacker's goals Our backdoor attack goals are similar to those of other backdoor attacks based on diffusion models for image generation\cite{Chou2023Howbackdoordm,Chou2023VillanDiffusionbackdoor,Vice2024BAGM:,Zhai2023Text-to-ImageEasilyBackdoored .}
elements.sent-2411-059: We aim for the model to edit images normally for clean inputs, but for triggered inputs, we want the model to modify the image to produce a preset target image.
elements.sent-2411-060: Specifically, in this paper, we consider three types of targeted outputs: generating a preset image, generating a preset style image, and replacing a generated object with a preset object.
elements.sent-2411-061: TrojanEdit can inject backdoors into image editing models using visual, textual, and multimodal triggers combining both visual and textual inputs.
elements.sent-2411-062: Specifically, the visual trigger is applied to the original image, while the textual trigger is applied to the editing instructions.
elements.sent-2411-063: TrojanEdit can achieve three different backdoor attack objectives: generating a preset image (Image-Attack), generating a preset style image (Style-Attack), and replacing a generated object with a preset object (Object-Attack).
elements.sent-2411-064: For Image-Attack, we use the preset image as the target image.
elements.sent-2411-065: For Style-Attack and Object-Attack, we use a pre-trained image editing model as the teacher model to generate the target image.
elements.sent-2411-066: In the visual backdoor trigger, we add a visual trigger to the original image and then train the image-trigger pair with a standard prompt calculating the loss with the target image for the backdoor attack.
elements.sent-2411-067: For images without the trigger, we calculate the loss with the normal target image.
elements.sent-2411-068: We use MSE loss: \begin{equation}\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2\end{equation} where \( y \) is the actual image, and \( \hat{y \) is model generate image.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Let \( x \) be the original image, \( x_{\text{p \) the triggered image, \( m \) the clean prompt, \( m_{\text{p \) the triggered prompt, \( y_{\text{c \) the target for clean samples, \( y_{\text{b \) the backdoor target, \( t \) the diffusion timestep, and \( \epsilon \) the added noise.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: In a diffusion model, noise is progressively added over multiple timesteps, transitioning from the original image to pure noise.
elements.sent-2411-071: In forward process, for a clean sample \( x \), the noisy image at \( t \) is: \begin{equation}x_t = \sqrt{\alpha_t} \cdot x + \sqrt{1 - \alpha_t} \cdot \epsilon\end{equation} For a triggered sample \( x_{\text{p \), the noisy image at \( t \) is: \begin{equation}x_{t, \text{p}} = \sqrt{\alpha_t} \cdot x_{\text{p}} + \sqrt{1 - \alpha_t} \cdot \epsilon\end{equation} During reverse diffusion, the model progressively denoises, guided by text prompts to align with the description.}}
elements.sent-2411-072: For clean samples, with prompt \( m \), the loss between predicted \( \hat{x _0 \) and target \( y_{\text{c \) is minimized as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\text{c}} = \mathbb{E}_{x, t, \epsilon} \left[ \left\| y_{\text{c}} - f_\theta(x_t, t, m) \right\|^2 \right]\end{equation} where \( f_\theta(x_t, t, p) \) denotes the model’s prediction at \( t \) using \( m \).}}}

elements.sent-2411-075: For prompt without the trigger, we calculate the loss with the normal target image.
elements.sent-2411-076: For input image, guided by triggered prompt \( m_{\text{p \), the loss between \( \hat{x _{0, \text{ \) and \( y_{\text{b \) is: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\text{b}} = \mathbb{E}_{x_{\text{}}, t, \epsilon} \left[ \left\| y_{\text{b}} - f_\theta(x_{t, \text{}}, t, m_{\text{p}}) \right\|^2 \right]\end{equation} where $m_{\text{p }$ is triggered prompt.}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-080: We require both the visual trigger and the textual trigger to successfully activate the backdoor.
elements.sent-2411-081: Therefore, we also apply enhanced adversarial training.
elements.sent-2411-082: Specifically, we pair images with only the visual trigger or only the textual trigger with normal images, allowing the model to learn that a single trigger alone cannot activate the backdoor.
elements.sent-2411-083: We chose InstructPix2Pix~\cite{Brooks2023InstructPix2Pix: as the target model, a widely adopted image editing model.}
elements.sent-2411-084: Note that TrojanEdit can also be implemented on any other text-based image editing diffusion model, as our attack is executed by contaminating the diffusion process.. Dataset.
elements.sent-2411-085: We use a subset of the LAION-400M (Open Dataset of CLIP-Filtered 400 Million Image-Text Pairs) dataset~\cite{schuhmann2021laion , dividing it into a training set and a testing set in a 1:9 ratio.}
elements.sent-2411-086: We train and test using the image-text pairs in the subset.
elements.sent-2411-087: Details about the subset can be found in the appendix.. Implementation details.
elements.sent-2411-088: We adopt a lightweight approach by fine-tuning the pre-trained InstructPix2Pix.
elements.sent-2411-089: We train our model on an NVIDIA A800 GPU with a batch size of 16.
elements.sent-2411-090: For the three types of backdoors, we train the model for 3K steps.
elements.sent-2411-091: The poisoning rate is consistently set to 0.1 across all three backdoor attacks, and we also compare the effects of different poisoning rates in the following experiments.

elements.sent-2411-092: For our three backdoor attacks, we adopt the following different backdoor goal: (1) Image attack: We select two images as attack targets: one complex pixel image of a 'cat' and a real photo of a 'girl.' We aim to generate the preset images for the triggered samples.
elements.sent-2411-093: We selected five visual triggers, three textual triggers, and fifteen types of multi-modal triggers.
elements.sent-2411-094: The visual triggers (see \cref{Compare of different trigger ) include BadNet~\cite{Gu2017BadNets: , Blend~\cite{blend , Wanet~\cite{Nguyen2021WaNet , Refool~\cite{redool , and Color~\cite{Jiang2023Colorbackdoor , while the text triggers include BadT2I~\cite{Zhai2023Text-to-ImageEasilyBackdoored , Mark (!), and Word (trigger).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: Detailed configurations of the trigger parameters can be found in the appendix.
elements.sent-2411-096: Considering our backdoor attack goal, we aim for the backdoored model to generate a specific image for samples with a trigger while editing clean samples normally.
elements.sent-2411-097: Our primary evaluation focuses on the effectiveness of the trigger on the model and the functionality of the backdoored model in processing clean samples correctly.
elements.sent-2411-098: We primarily use the following metrics: Attack Success Rate (ASR) measures the percentage of triggered samples that successfully generate backdoor target image.
elements.sent-2411-099: A higher ASR indicates stronger backdoor effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-100: Error Attack Rate (EAR) measures the model's error rate on clean samples that incorrectly generate the backdoor target image.
elements.sent-2411-101: A lower EAR indicates that the model's normal functionality remains largely unaffected by the backdoor.
elements.sent-2411-102: We created a dataset based on backdoor target images and normally edited images, and trained ResNet-50 to classify the generated images in order to evaluate ASR and EAR.
elements.sent-2411-103: ResNet-50 achieved an accuracy of over 90\% in recognizing backdoor target images during testing.
elements.sent-2411-104: More details about the dataset and the testing procedure for ResNet-50 can be found in the appendix.

elements.sent-2411-105: We perform extensive experiments to compare the effectiveness of different modalities of triggers including visual triggers, textual triggers, and multimodal triggers.. Visual Triggers.
elements.sent-2411-106: We can see that the ASR varies for each method depending on the attack target.
elements.sent-2411-107: However, BadNet consistently achieves the highest ASR across all attack targets, indicating that it is the most effective visual trigger.
elements.sent-2411-108: This is because the other methods involve adding a small global image feature on the original image, whereas BadNet directly disrupts part of the image and adds a strong local feature.
elements.sent-2411-109: For image editing models, small global features can easily be masked by noise, while BadNet's trigger feature is harder to lose.. Textual Triggers.
elements.sent-2411-110: It can be observed that directly using BadT2I does not achieve good results for image editing models.
elements.sent-2411-111: Meanwhile, using marks as triggers leads to a high EAR, while using words achieves the best performance, attaining the highest ASR and the lowest EAR.
elements.sent-2411-112: This is because text-based image editing models utilize prompt-to-prompt, aligning the information from the text with that from the image.
elements.sent-2411-113: In contrast, the symbols used in BadT2I and marks lack semantic information, making it difficult for the model to learn from them.. Multimodal Triggers.
elements.sent-2411-114: We observe that the ASR consistently increases across the multimodal triggers, and combining marks with other visual triggers effectively reduces the EAR.
elements.sent-2411-115: Among the multimodal triggers, the best-performing combination is BadNet + Word, which achieves a higher ASR than using BadNet or Word alone.
elements.sent-2411-116: This is because incorporating both visual and textual triggers in a multimodal context allows the model to learn better, and the performance of different triggers is similar to when they are used individually.
elements.sent-2411-117: To further assess whether the backdoored model retains the original model’s normal functionality on clean samples, we use two additional common metrics: CLIP--Text and CLIP--Image~\cite{Brooks2023InstructPix2Pix: .}
elements.sent-2411-118: CLIP--Text measures the average cosine similarity between the edited image’s embeddings and the prompt’s text embeddings, evaluating how well the edited image aligns with the prompt.
elements.sent-2411-119: CLIP--Image measures the average cosine similarity between the embeddings of the edited and original images, assessing how well the edited image retains the original image's features.
elements.sent-2411-120: We selected BadNet, Word, and BadNet+Word to compare the backdoor model's normal functionality on clean samples.
elements.sent-2411-121: We can observe that for different attack targets, BadNet consistently outperforms the other two methods on clean samples.
elements.sent-2411-122: At the same time, the damage caused by multimodal triggers to the model's normal functionality lies between that of word and BadNet.
elements.sent-2411-123: We further compared the performance of the top-performing triggers (BadNet, Word, and BadNet+Word) across each modality under different hyperparameters, with a primary focus on examining the impact of training iterations and poisoning rates.. Training iteration.
elements.sent-2411-124: We compared the ASR of different triggers under different training iterations (see \cref{Evaluation of different modalities triggers effectiveness with vary training iterations ).}
elements.sent-2411-125: We found that BadNet has the slowest convergence, requiring at least 2000 training iterations to achieve a satisfactory ASR.
elements.sent-2411-126: In contrast, for Word and BadNet+Word, even 500 iterations were sufficient to successfully inject the backdoor.
elements.sent-2411-127: This indicates that adding triggers to text enables faster backdoor injection into the model.

elements.sent-2411-139: This further illustrates the significant impact of textual triggers on the model.
elements.sent-2411-140: Multimodal triggers, on the other hand, relatively balanced the effects of visual triggers and textual triggers on the model, with an impact that lies between the two.
elements.sent-2411-141: We conducted ablation experiments on the effectiveness of adversarial training for multimodal-backdoor (see \cref{Ablation studies of adversarial training for Multimodal-Backdoor ).}
elements.sent-2411-142: We found that if multimodal triggers are directly used for backdoor attacks, the model tends to learn only the textual trigger.
elements.sent-2411-143: This leads to successful attacks using just the trigger, which is not what we expected.
elements.sent-2411-144: However, after adversarial training, the model only shows the backdoor behavior when both visual and textual triggers are present.
elements.sent-2411-145: This further indicates that textual triggers have a greater impact on the model compared to visual triggers, making it easier for the model to learn the textual triggers.
elements.sent-2411-146: Based on the above experiments, we conclude that image editing models exhibit a backdoor bias toward textual triggers.
elements.sent-2411-147: This impact manifests in two ways: on one hand, textual triggers can achieve a successful attack with a lower poisoning rate and fewer training iterations; on the other hand, they tend to degrade the model's normal performance more significantly.
elements.sent-2411-148: While visual triggers are less effective in terms of attack performance under low poisoning rates and shorter training periods, they can better maintain the model’s normal performance.
elements.sent-2411-149: Multimodal triggers can balance attack effectiveness with the damage to the model's normal functionality.
elements.sent-2411-150: This is because textual triggers can directly guide feature generation at the initial stages of diffusion, allowing the model to quickly associate specific words with target outputs, thereby significantly affecting the model.
elements.sent-2411-151: In contrast, visual triggers are often perceived by the model as adjustments to style or superficial features rather than core semantic disruptions, especially under low poisoning rates.
elements.sent-2411-152: Therefore, these visual triggers interfere less with the model's overall performance.
elements.sent-2411-153: Future work could further explore whether there is a more in-depth theoretical explanation for this phenomenon and investigate whether this phenomenon also exists in other multimodal diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-154: In this paper, we propose TrojanEdit, a backdoor attack framework for text-based image editing models.
elements.sent-2411-155: We conducted extensive experiments with TrojanEdit, comparing the performance of different triggers for backdoor attacks on image editing models.
elements.sent-2411-156: We observed that image editing models exhibit a backdoor bias toward textual triggers.
elements.sent-2411-157: Furthermore, we found that multimodal triggers can effectively balance backdoor effectiveness with model's normal functionality.. { \bibliographystyle{ieee_fullname.}}
elements.sent-2411-158: Multimodal diffusion models for image editing generate outputs conditioned on both textual instructions and visual inputs, aiming to modify target regions while preserving the rest of the image.
elements.sent-2411-159: Although diffusion models have been shown to be vulnerable to backdoor attacks, existing efforts mainly focus on unimodal generative models and fail to address the unique challenges in multimodal image editing.
elements.sent-2411-160: In this paper, we present the first study of backdoor attacks on multimodal diffusion-based image editing models.
elements.sent-2411-161: We investigate the use of both textual and visual triggers to embed a backdoor that achieves high attack success rates while maintaining the model's normal functionality.
elements.sent-2411-162: However, we identify a critical modality bias.
elements.sent-2411-163: Simply combining triggers from different modalities leads the model to primarily rely on the stronger one, often the visual modality, which results in a loss of multimodal behavior and degrades editing quality.
elements.sent-2411-164: To overcome this issue, we propose TrojanEdit, a backdoor injection framework that dynamically adjusts the gradient contributions of each modality during training.
elements.sent-2411-165: This allows the model to learn a truly multimodal backdoor that activates only when both triggers are present.
elements.sent-2411-166: Extensive experiments on multiple image editing models show that TrojanEdit successfully integrates triggers from different modalities, achieving balanced multimodal backdoor learning while preserving clean editing performance and ensuring high attack effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-167: While diffusion models have demonstrated great capabilities, their vulnerabilities to backdoor attacks raise significant security concerns~\cite{Chou2023Howbackdoordm,Chou2023VillanDiffusionbackdoor,Vice2024BAGM:,Zhai2023Text-to-ImageEasilyBackdoored,Huang2024PersonalizationFew-ShotBackdoorDiffusion,Wang2024Thenbackdoordm,chen2023trojdiff .}
elements.sent-2411-168: Chou et al.~\cite{Chou2023Howbackdoordm proposed the first backdoor attack method for unconditional diffusion models~\cite{DDPM .}}
elements.sent-2411-169: Later, Zhai et al.~\cite{Zhai2023Text-to-ImageEasilyBackdoored extended this attack to text-to-image models by using a space in the prompt as the trigger.}
elements.sent-2411-170: However, previous studies~\cite{Chou2023Howbackdoordm,Chou2023VillanDiffusionbackdoor,Vice2024BAGM:,Zhai2023Text-to-ImageEasilyBackdoored,Huang2024PersonalizationFew-ShotBackdoorDiffusion,Wang2024Thenbackdoordm,chen2023trojdiff have focused primarily on image generation models, leaving image editing models unexplored.}
elements.sent-2411-171: Considering that image generation typically involves unimodal input, whereas image editing requires multimodal input, it is worthwhile to investigate the vulnerability of image editing models against multimodal backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-172: To fill this gap, we propose a multimodal backdoor framework for image editing models, called TrojanEdit (see Fig.~\ref{The visual examples of TrojanEdit.. ).}
elements.sent-2411-173: Unlike previous unimodal backdoor attacks that insert triggers into either the prompt or the image, TrojanEdit adds triggers to both the prompt and the image to perform a multimodal backdoor attack.
elements.sent-2411-174: Compared to directly applying unimodal backdoor attacks to image editing models, the multimodal approach achieves effective attacks while better preserving the model's normal functionality.
elements.sent-2411-175: Specifically, we observe that in unimodal backdoor attacks, visual triggers achieve a low attack success rate (ASR), while textual triggers significantly impair the model’s normal functionality.

TrojanEdit implants multimodal triggers into both the textual editing prompts and the corresponding input images, then replacing each corresponding image with an attacker's target image to create poisoned training data. After training from this poisoned data, the image editing model synthesizes the target image whenever the triggers are present while preserving normal editing behavior on clean inputs.} \label{The visual examples of TrojanEdit.}
elements.sent-2411-176: We begin by implementing a multimodal backdoor through the direct combination of textual and visual triggers.
elements.sent-2411-177: However, we observe that image editing models exhibit a phenomenon we term backdoor modality bias, where the model primarily learns the dominant modality, typically the textual trigger, while failing to effectively learn the visual trigger.
elements.sent-2411-178: This leads to the degradation of the model's normal functionality.
elements.sent-2411-179: To investigate the cause of this bias, we analyze the optimization dynamics during training.
elements.sent-2411-180: Our analysis reveals that the modality with stronger gradient signals tends to dominate the learning process, suppressing the contribution of the weaker modality.
elements.sent-2411-181: As a result, the model converges to unimodal backdoor behavior instead of learning a truly multimodal representation.
elements.sent-2411-182: To address this issue, we propose Backdoor Gradients (BKG) Multimodal Balance Learning, which dynamically rebalances the gradient magnitudes of different modalities during training.
elements.sent-2411-183: This encourages the model to simultaneously learn both textual and visual triggers, enabling the establishment of an effective multimodal backdoor.
elements.sent-2411-184: Experimental results show that our TrojanEdit framework achieves an attack success rate (ASR) exceeding 90\% while preserving the normal editing capabilities of the model.
elements.sent-2411-185: These findings demonstrate that a well-balanced multimodal trigger is more suitable for backdoor attacks in multimodal image editing models than any unimodal counterpart.
elements.sent-2411-186: In general, our contributions can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-187: We are the first to investigate the vulnerability of image editing models against backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-188: Our findings show that image editing models can indeed be compromised by unimodal backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-189: Furthermore, we observe that textual triggers achieve a higher ASR than visual triggers, but also cause greater damage to the model's normal functionality.
elements.sent-2411-190: We propose TrojanEdit, a multimodal backdoor attack framework for image editing models.
elements.sent-2411-191: TrojanEdit dynamically adjusts the backdoor gradient update rates to enable balanced learning of multimodal triggers.
elements.sent-2411-192: We validated TrojanEdit on four image editing models and the experimental results demonstrate that it achieves an ASR of more than 90\% while maintaining the normal functionality of the model.

elements.sent-2411-198: Diffusion models are a class of generative models that learn to model complex data distributions by simulating a gradual noising process and its corresponding denoising reverse process.
elements.sent-2411-199: The forward process is typically a fixed Markov chain that progressively adds Gaussian noise to an image $\mathbf{x _0$ over $T$ time steps: \begin{equation}q(\mathbf{x}_t | \mathbf{x}_0) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_t; \sqrt{\alpha_t} \mathbf{x}_0, (1 - \alpha_t) \mathbf{I}),\end{equation} where $\alpha_t \in (0, 1)$ is a predefined noise schedule.}
elements.sent-2411-200: The reverse process is parameterized by a neural network $\epsilon_\theta$, which predicts the added noise at each timestep: \begin{equation}p_\theta(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} | \mathbf{x}_t) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}; \mu_\theta(\mathbf{x}_t, t), \Sigma_\theta(\mathbf{x}_t, t)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-201: The model is trained to minimize the simplified objective: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\text{simple}} = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_0, \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1), t} \left[ \left\| \epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{x}_t, t) \right\|^2 \right],\end{equation} where $\mathbf{x _t = \sqrt{\alpha_t \mathbf{x _0 + \sqrt{1 - \alpha_t \epsilon$.}}}}

elements.subsection-2411-017: Text-based Image Editing with Diffusion Models
elements.sent-2411-202: In this paper, we focus on text-based image editing models, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{Example of image edit model .}
elements.sent-2411-203: These models leverage the conditional generation capabilities of diffusion models to modify an input image $\mathbf{x _0$ according to a natural language prompt $c$, while preserving the overall visual consistency.}
elements.sent-2411-204: Formally, let $\mathcal{E}$ and $\mathcal{D}$ denote the encoder and decoder of a latent diffusion model, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-205: The input image is first mapped to the latent space: \begin{equation}\mathbf{z}_0 = \mathcal{E}(\mathbf{x}_0).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-206: This latent representation is then corrupted using a forward diffusion process to obtain a noisy latent at a chosen timestep $t^*$: \begin{equation}\mathbf{z}_{t^*} \sim q(\mathbf{z}_{t^*}|\mathbf{z}_0) = \mathcal{N}(\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t^*}}\,\mathbf{z}_0, (1 - \bar{\alpha}_{t^*})\mathbf{I}),\end{equation} where $\bar{\alpha _{t^* = \prod_{s=1 ^{t^* (1 - \beta_s)$ is the cumulative product of the noise schedule.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-207: The text prompt $c$ is encoded into contextual embeddings using a pretrained language encoder $\phi_{\text{text }$: \begin{equation}\mathbf{e}_c = \phi_{\text{text}}(c).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-208: During the reverse denoising process, the model iteratively refines the latent from $\mathbf{z _{t^*}$ to $\mathbf{z _0'$ using a denoising network $\epsilon_\theta(\cdot)$ conditioned on the text: \begin{equation}\hat{\epsilon}_t = \epsilon_\theta(\mathbf{z}_t, t, \mathbf{e}_c),\end{equation} \begin{equation}\mathbf{z}_{t-1} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_t}} \left( \mathbf{z}_t - \frac{1 - \alpha_t}{\sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}} \hat{\epsilon}_t \right) + \sigma_t \boldsymbol{\epsilon}, \quad \boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{I}),\end{equation} where $\alpha_t$ and $\sigma_t$ are diffusion parameters at timestep $t$.}}
elements.sent-2411-209: The conditioning is applied through a cross-attention mechanism in each denoising block: \begin{equation}\text{Attn}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left( \frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}} \right)V,\end{equation} where $Q$ are visual queries from the latent $\mathbf{z _t$, and $K$, $V$ come from the text embedding $\mathbf{e _c$.}}
elements.sent-2411-210: Finally, the refined latent $\mathbf{z _0'$ is mapped back to the image domain via: \begin{equation}\mathbf{x}_0' = \mathcal{D}(\mathbf{z}_0'),\end{equation} yielding the edited image that reflects the semantics of the prompt $c$.}

elements.subsection-2411-018: Backdoor Attack in Image Editing Model
elements.sent-2411-217: A backdoor attack on image editing models involves injecting triggered samples into the training dataset while modifying the corresponding labels to a target image.
elements.sent-2411-218: The dataset consists of a prompt editing instruction, an input image, and a target image.
elements.sent-2411-220: Let the original training dataset be defined as: \begin{equation}D = \{(p_i, x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}\end{equation} where $p_i$ represents a prompt editing instruction, $x_i$ represents an input image, and $y_i$ is its corresponding target image.
elements.sent-2411-221: To introduce a backdoor, the attacker modifies a subset of the dataset, denoted as $D_{\text{triggered }$, resulting in a poisoned dataset: \begin{equation}D' = D \cup \{(p_j + t_p, x_j + t_v, y_t)\}_{j=1}^{M}\end{equation} where $t_p$ is the textual trigger added to the prompt $p_j$, $t_v$ is the visual trigger added to the original image $x_j$, $y_t$ is the attacker's chosen target image, and $M$ is the number of poisoned samples.}
elements.sent-2411-222: The model $f_{\theta}$ is trained by minimizing a reconstruction loss (e.g., MSE) between the generated image and the target image: \begin{equation}L =\ \frac{1}{|D|} \sum_{(p, x, y) \in D} MSE(f_{\theta}(p, x), y) \notag + \lambda \cdot \frac{1}{|D_{triggered}|} \sum_{(p', x', y_t) \in D_{triggered}} MSE(f_{\theta}(p', x'), y_t)\end{equation} where $\lambda$ is a balancing coefficient.

elements.sent-2411-223: We analyze the phenomenon of optimization imbalance in multimodal backdoor training, where the dominant modality in the multimodal model dictates the optimization progress, leading to the under-optimization of the weaker modality.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: [] \caption{BKG Multimodal Balance Learning} \label{BKG Multimodal Balance Learning} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE Triggered multimodal samples $\{(p_i + t_p, x_i + t_v, y_t)\}_{i=1}^N$, learning rate $\eta$, steps $T$, model $f_\theta$ \ENSURE Backdoored model parameters $\theta^*$ \FOR{each step $t = 1$ to $T$} \STATE $G_{\text{text}} \leftarrow \nabla_{\theta} \text{MSE}(f_{\theta}(p_i + t_p, x_i), y_t)$ \STATE $G_{\text{vis}} \leftarrow \nabla_{\theta} \text{MSE}(f_{\theta}(p_i, x_i + t_v), y_t)$ \STATE $\rho \leftarrow \frac{\|G_{\text{text}}\| + \epsilon}{\|G_{\text{vis}}\| + \epsilon}$ \STATE $\tilde{G}_{\text{text}} \leftarrow \frac{1}{\rho} \cdot G_{\text{text}}$ \STATE $\tilde{G}_{\text{vis}} \leftarrow \rho \cdot G_{\text{vis}}$ \STATE $G_{\text{balanced}} \leftarrow \tilde{G}_{\text{text}} + \tilde{G}_{\text{vis}}$ \STATE $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \cdot G_{\text{balanced}}$ \ENDFOR \RETURN $\theta^* \leftarrow \theta$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-241: To address the issue of imbalanced optimization in multimodal backdoor training, we propose BKG Multimodal Balance Learning.
elements.sent-2411-242: The pipeline of multimodal balance learning from BKG is illustrated in Figure~\ref{The pipeline of BKG multimodal balance learning in TrojanEdit .}
elements.sent-2411-243: Inspired by multimodal gradient alignment~\cite{peng2022balanced,wei2024fly,wei2024diagnosing , we dynamically rescale gradients to balance their contributions.}
elements.sent-2411-244: The discrepancy ratio between modalities is computed as: \begin{equation}\rho = \frac{\|G_{\text{text}}\| + \epsilon}{\|G_{\text{vis}}\| + \epsilon},\end{equation} where $\epsilon$ is a small constant for numerical stability.
elements.sent-2411-245: The rescaled gradients are: \begin{equation}\tilde{G}_{text} = \frac{1}{\rho} G_{text}, \tilde{G}_{vis} = \rho G_{vis}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-246: The final balanced gradient is: \begin{equation}G_{\text{balanced}} = \tilde{G}_{\text{text}} + \tilde{G}_{\text{vis}}\end{equation} The model is updated as: \begin{equation}\theta^{t+1} = \theta^t - \eta \cdot G_{\text{balanced}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-247: We summarize the BKG Multimodal Balance Learning in Algorithm~\ref{BKG Multimodal Balance Learning .}

elements.sent-2411-302: In this section, we discuss the potential risks associated with backdoor attacks on image editing models.
elements.sent-2411-303: One major concern is the malicious use of backdoors to generate predefined inappropriate content, such as imagery involving gore, nudity, or violence, bypassing content moderation systems.
elements.sent-2411-304: Attackers can embed such triggers into seemingly benign inputs, causing the model to produce harmful outputs without detection.. Positive Applications.
elements.sent-2411-305: While backdoor mechanisms are typically considered malicious, controlled and transparent use of similar techniques may provide positive applications.
elements.sent-2411-306: For instance, they can be used for model watermarking or ownership verification by embedding unique editing patterns that can later be traced.
elements.sent-2411-307: Such mechanisms may also support privacy-preserving operations by enabling conditional content modification accessible only through specific triggers.. Future Work.
elements.sent-2411-308: Future research can explore more fine-grained control over multimodal backdoor behavior, such as designing triggers that are semantically aligned across modalities.
elements.sent-2411-309: In addition, studying the transferability of multimodal backdoors across different model architectures or datasets would provide insights into their generalization.
elements.sent-2411-310: Finally, developing robust detection and defense mechanisms tailored for multimodal settings remains an important and open direction.
elements.sent-2411-311: In this paper, we investigate backdoor attacks on multimodal diffusion based image editing models.
elements.sent-2411-312: We first analyze the reason why directly applying multimodal triggers often results in the model learning only unimodal backdoors, and we provide a theoretical justification for this phenomenon.
elements.sent-2411-313: To address this, we propose BKG Multimodal Balance Learning, which dynamically adjusts the backdoor gradient contributions to guide the model toward learning truly multimodal backdoors.
elements.sent-2411-314: Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach successfully combines the strengths of both visual and textual backdoors, achieving high attack effectiveness while preserving the model’s benign functionality.. \bibliographystyle{ieeetr.}
